coredata.dc:title: A POM/viologen-based supramolecular fluorescent probe for Ag+ detection and application of visible hydrogel based intelligent sensing system
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Kai
Liu, Tao
Ying, Jun
Tian, Aixiang
Wang, Xiuli
coredata.dc:description: Silver (Ag + ) as a typical heavy metal ion has significant impacts on human health, so monitoring Ag + level is an important issue. In this work, a new POM (polyoxometalates)/viologen-based supramolecular compound, [(HMSBP)2(γ-Mo8O26)]·4H2O (1) has been synthesized by hydrothermal method (MSBP = 1-(4-Methanesulfonyl-benzyl)-[4,4']bipyridinyl-1-ium). This POM/viologen-based supramolecular compound exhibits the unique supramolecular structure, excellent optical stability and superior fluorescence intensity, which is expected to become a promising fluorescent sensor. The coordination reaction between SO, pyridine N atom and Ag+ in compound 1 makes the detection of Ag+ achieved and the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.0411 μM in the range of 0.1–3 μM. Moreover, this supramolecular fluorescent probe has successfully realized the quantitative monitoring of Ag + concentration in environmental water samples and commercially available disinfectant spray. Viologen is regarded as a good external stimulus responsive material, while its discoloration behavior can be exploited as an intelligent sensor system. The platform based on hydrogel and smartphones realizes the visualization and real-time quantification of Ag+, which provides an effective way for the rapid and convenient determination of Ag+. This provides a feasible way to promote the qualitative/semi quantitative detection of visual fluorescence POM/viologen sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ag+ detection
POM/viologen supramolecular fluorescent probe
Quantitative determination
Intelligent sensor system

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Ag+ in practical samples
Optimization parameter and fluorescence response to Ag+
Possible quenching mechanism of compound 1 to Ag+
Quantification of Ag+ in practical samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation procedure and schematic diagram of the application of compound 1.

(a) The asymmetrical unit of compound 1. (b) The SBU of [γ-Mo8O26]4− polyoxoanion. (c) 2D network constructed by hydrogen bonding (N1−H⋯O1W and O1W−H⋯O8). (d) 2D network constructed by hydrogen bonding (along the bc axis).
(a) PXRD patterns of compound 1 and compound 1+Ag + . (b) FT-IR spectra of compound 1 and compound 1+Ag + .

Characterization of optical properties of compound 1: (a) UV–vis, excitation and emission fluorescence spectra (excitation at 395 nm, emission at 457 nm). (b) Emission spectra at different wavelengths of excitation. (c) Fluorescence decay curves. (d) Fluorescence stability at different storage times, (e) pH = 2−12 and (f) Different UV lamp irradiation times.

(a) Influence of interferents on the fluorescence response of compound 1 toward Ag+. Optimization of conditions for detecting Ag+: (b) Time of recognition reaction, (c) Temperature and (d) pH. (e) Fluorescence spectra of compound 1 upon addition of different concentrations of Ag+ (0.1−20 μM). (f) Plot of I/I0 versus Ag+ concentration (inset: the linear relationship between I/I0 and Ag+ concentration ranged from 0.1 to 3 μM).

Characterization of the sensing mechanism of compound 1 for Ag+: (a) Fluorescence decay curves, (b) UV–vis, (c) XPS of compound 1 and (d-f) High-resolution spectrum split peak fits of Ag3d, N1s and O1s, respectively.

(a) Diagram illustrating the process of detecting Ag+ by smart sensing systems. Photographs of hydrogel sensor under different light sources (0−5 μM Ag+): (b) Natural light, (c) 365 nm UV light. (d) Linear relationship between H/V values and Ag+ concentration. (e) Relationship between storage time of hydrogel sensor and H/V values.
Measurement of Ag+ concentration in practical sample and water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The disinfectant spray was diluted 100-fold before use.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Measurement of Ag+ concentration in disinfectant spray solution by compound 1+hydrogel sensor.

head.ce:title: A POM/viologen-based supramolecular fluorescent probe for Ag+ detection and application of visible hydrogel based intelligent sensing system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Bohai University, Jinzhou, 121013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Bohai University, Jinzhou 121013, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Sciences, North China University of Science and Technology, Tangshan, Hebei, 063210, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: North China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Sciences, North China University of Science and Technology, Tangshan, Hebei 063210, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silver (Ag + ) as a typical heavy metal ion has significant impacts on human health, so monitoring Ag + level is an important issue. In this work, a new POM (polyoxometalates)/viologen-based supramolecular compound, [(HMSBP)2(γ-Mo8O26)]·4H2O (1) has been synthesized by hydrothermal method (MSBP = 1-(4-Methanesulfonyl-benzyl)-[4,4']bipyridinyl-1-ium). This POM/viologen-based supramolecular compound exhibits the unique supramolecular structure, excellent optical stability and superior fluorescence intensity, which is expected to become a promising fluorescent sensor. The coordination reaction between SO, pyridine N atom and Ag+ in compound 1 makes the detection of Ag+ achieved and the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.0411 μM in the range of 0.1–3 μM. Moreover, this supramolecular fluorescent probe has successfully realized the quantitative monitoring of Ag + concentration in environmental water samples and commercially available disinfectant spray. Viologen is regarded as a good external stimulus responsive material, while its discoloration behavior can be exploited as an intelligent sensor system. The platform based on hydrogel and smartphones realizes the visualization and real-time quantification of Ag+, which provides an effective way for the rapid and convenient determination of Ag+. This provides a feasible way to promote the qualitative/semi quantitative detection of visual fluorescence POM/viologen sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The extravagant release of heavy metal ions prompted by manufacturing has an enormous impact on human health and ecosystem pollution [1,2]. Among them, silver ion (Ag + ) is one of the main targets of a series of heavy metal ion families and it is well-known for the widespread applications in chemical engineering and the photography industry [3,4]. Hitherto, researchers have found that Ag + also holds an extremely important position in the field of biopharmaceuticals, because of its effective tissue coalescence and antibiotic capabilities [5,6]. Despite the wide application of Ag+, its potential toxicity cannot be ignored. Simultaneously, wastes containing Ag + are discharged into the ecosystem, and the accumulated Ag + seriously endangers the food chain. Long-term exposure to high Ag+ levels can lead to the outbreak of many diseases, such as skin irritation, nerve injury, hepatotoxicity, stomach pain, seizures, and kidney damage [7–11]. Moreover, it has been reported that Ag+ has strong coordination ability, which can quickly and tightly bind and inactivate thiol enzymes in organisms to threaten human metabolism [12,13]. Therefore, it is urgent for environmental science to accurately detect the concentration of Ag+ in actual samples. According to the standards of U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and World Health Organization (WHO), the maximum concentration of Ag+ in drinking water is 460 nM [14]. Thus, it is of great significance to detect Ag+ in environmental monitoring systems.

Up to present, conventional methods such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), inductively coupled plasma emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) and X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy (XRFS) techniques have been used for the detection of Ag + [15–18]. Unfortunately, even though these methods are described as having outstanding sensitivity, their low selectivity, high cost and long pretreatment time seriously hinder their widespread application in production field. Fluorescent probe has the characteristics of simple technical requirements, low cost, good portability, high sensitivity, good selectivity, real-time detection, non-invasive detection and so on, which shows great potential in analyte detection under different environmental factors [19,20]. Furthermore, the visual detection system is utilized in fluorescence methods, which is conducive to the development of quantitative analysis equipment. The fluorescent probes with Ag+ detection have been developed by researchers and with the majority of sensing mechanisms are coordination interactions [21–24]. Different from other mechanisms, coordination reaction based fluorescent probes are preferred. The irreversible chemical reaction between these probes and the target is rapid and lasting, which can be explored by spectral results to promote the mastery of the reaction principle [25–27].

Supramolecular compounds with excellent reversibility and sensitivity have been widely used to construct stimulus-responsive fluorescent materials for various applications, such as monitors, sensors and information security [28–30]. In addition, the rich supramolecular interactions, including host-guest interactions, ligand-metal compounds, π-π stacking, ion interactions, and hydrogen bonding, not only provide flexibility for optical imaging, but also have good self-assembly ability [31–33]. Therefore, supramolecular compounds can be served as fluorescent probes with more exceptional performance. Viologen is a type of multifunctional stimulus response material that can respond to external stimuli and change their color or properties, which is widely used in fields such as smart windows, information storage, energy conversion and inkless printing [34–36]. Supramolecular compounds based on POM/viologen also inherit the properties of viologen ligands, which can respond to UV light, X-rays, and potential. Especially representing discoloration that can be distinguished by the naked eye [37–39]. It can more conveniently identify the visual discoloration of heavy metal ions, which makes it more advantageous as fluorescent probes.

In this work, a novel viologen-based supramolecular compound [(HMSBP)2(γ-Mo8O26)]·4H2O (1) (MSBP = 1-(4-Methanesulfonyl-benzyl)-[4,4']bipyridinyl-1-ium) is synthesized by hydrothermal method. It is found that compound 1 has the unique supramolecular structure, superior fluorescence intensity, good biocompatibility and stability under different conditions, which makes it have great advantages as a supramolecular fluorescent probe in the practical application of intelligent sensing systems. There are abundant surface functional groups (SO, pyridine N atom) in compound 1, and Ag+ can coordinate with its surface functional groups to realize the fluorescence “on-off” process. Compound 1+hydrogel has been successfully employed in solid-state sensors for trace Ag + recognition. Meanwhile, compound 1+hydrogel system connected with smartphones can accurately quantify the results by programming to recognize the discoloration of fluorescence, which is beneficial to production and life applications (Fig. 1 ). With low cost, convenient use and high sensitivity, the intelligent sensing system has broad application prospects, which lays a foundation for the intelligent fluorescence sensing system to enter human life. It promotes the potential development of visual fluorescence sensors for quantitative detection with naked eyes.

Acrylamide (AAM), ammonium persulphate, NH4MoO4, and 1-(4-Methanesulfonyl-benzyl)-[4,4']bipyridinyl-1-ium (HMSBP) were supplied by Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. Silver nitrate and other salts were all purchased from Kelong Reagent Co., Ltd. All organic reagents were bought from RunZe Reagent Co., Ltd. Disinfectant spray (trade name: Oxytech) was purchased from local drugstore (Jinzhou, Liaoning, China).

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) was measured by using an Ultima IV X-ray powder diffractometer (Kurary, Tokyo, Japan) at 40 kV and 40 mA through Cu Kα radiation (k = 1.5406 Å). Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared products were performed by a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer and PerkinElmer luminescence spectrometer (LS-55). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to identify the functional groups of the products by a Shimadzu IR Affinity 1S spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement for elemental component analysis of the production was conducted by an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The single crystal X-ray was measured on a Bruker APEX-IV CCD diffractometer with graphite monochromatic Mo Ka radiation at 298 K, and the structure was solved by direct method and refined with the full matrix least-squares method on F
2 embedded in SHELXTL program through using the Olex2 as the graphical interface. All hydrogen atoms were placed geometrically in ideal positions with a riding model, and all non-hydrogen atoms were refined by the anisotropic thermal parameters during the final cycles. CCDC 2292860 contains the crystallographic data for this paper.

A mixture of 0.05 mmol (17.3 mg) of HMSBP, 0.15 mmol (26.7 mg) of NH4MoO4, and 15 mL deionized water in a closed 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave (pH = 3.5 by HCl) was heated at 130 °C for 7 days and then cooled to room temperature naturally. Colorless block-shaped crystals were collected by filtration, and the yield was 58 % (based on MSBP). Anal. Calcd (%) for C36H44Mo8N4O34S2: C 22.66, H 2.32, N 2.94 %. Found: C 22.72, H 2.36, N 2.98. IR (KBr, cm−1): ν 3325, 3047, 2991, 2983, 2370, 1631, 1417, 1298, 1151, 926, 852, 672 (Fig. 3b).

Detection of Ag + by compound 1: (1) Selectivity: 0.5 mg of compound 1 was mixed separately with 0.1 mL solution of interference cations (Li + , Na + , K + , Ag + , Cu+, Hg2+ , Pd2+ , Fe2+ , Ca2+ , Mg2+ , Cd2+ , Mn2+ , Co2+ , Ni2+ , Zn2+ , Pb2+ , Cu2+ , Cr3+ , Al3+ , Fe3+ , Sn4+ ) (1 μM), then replenished to 3 mL with deionized water (obtaining experimental results) and shaken well. (2) Competition: 0.5 mg of compound 1 was mixed separately with 0.1 mL of Ag+ (1 μM) solution, and then 5 times of interference cation solution was added to it, which was fixed to 3 mL with deionized water and fully shaken. All fluorescence data of the mixtures were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Ag+ in practical samples
ce:section.ce:para: Samples of lake and tap water were respectively collected from lake in Bohai University and our laboratory (Jinzhou, Liaoning, China), which were heated to boiling and filtered to remove impurities. The commercial disinfectant spray was deliquated with 100 times by deionized water. The concentration of Ag+ can be determined by the standard curve sensed by compound 1.

0.15 g of compound 1 was added to 20 ml of distilled water and stirred. Then, 2 g of AAM and 0.1 g of ammonium persulfate were added to the above solution and subjected to ultrasonic treatment in an ice water bath for 2 min. Poured the mixture into the hydrogel mold and sonicated for 2 min to remove bubbles. The reaction was executed in an oven at 65 °C and the fluorescent hydrogel based on compound 1 was prepared. These hydrogels were immersed in solutions containing different concentrations of Ag + for 2 min, and recorded the color response of supramolecular fluorescent probe based on hydrogel under natural light and 365 nm portable UV light through smartphones for subsequent visual quantitative testing.

Single crystal X-ray diffraction shows that compound 1 belongs to a monoclinic crystal system with a spatial group of P21/n. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the asymmetric unit consists of two semi-isolated protonated MSBP + , a dissociative [γ-Mo8O26]4− polyoxoanion, and four crystal H2O molecules. [γ-Mo8O26]4− polyoxoanion is a typical isopolymolybdate anion, which is covalently connected by six {MoO6} octahedrons and two {MoO5} square pyramidal geometry. [γ-Mo8O26]4 − polyoxoanion forms a secondary structural unit (SBU), and the bond lengths of Mo–O range from 1.692(2) to 2.4294(19) Å (Fig. 2b). A 2D hydrogen-bonded network structure is constructed by MSBP + organic cations through hydrogen bonds (N1−H⋯O1W and O1W−H⋯O8) among one N atom of the pyridine ring, crystalliferous H2O molecules, one oxygen atom of the [γ-Mo8O26]4− polyoxoanion (N⋯O1W distance is 2.813 Å, O1W⋯O8 distance is 2.902 Å) (Fig. 2c and d). Other specific crystal database and the details are saved in Table S1−S3.

The phase purity of compound 1 is determined by powder X-ray diffraction analysis (PXRD) (Fig. 3a). The diffraction peaks of the as-synthesized compound 1 match the simulated peaks from the single crystal analysis, displaying excellent phase purity and crystallinity of compound 1. In addition, the composition of functional groups in compound 1 can be determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). (Fig. 3b). Evidently, compound 1 has a faint narrow peak at 2983 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of C−H, and 3325 cm−1 broad peak in compound 1 is N−H stretching vibration on pyridine after protonation of viologen ligand [40]. The peak at 1631 cm−1 is caused by stretching vibrations of CC/CN, while the SO peak is observed at 1151 and 1298 cm−1 [41]. In order to further explore the stretching vibration of SO, the characteristic peaks of SO are displayed at 1142, 1168 and 1292 cm−1 in Raman spectrum (Fig. S1) [42]. Furthermore, the sharp absorption bands at 672, 852, 926 cm−1 of compound 1 is corresponding to the [γ-Mo8O26]4− stretching vibration peaks [43].

Following special chemical structural characteristics of compound 1, it is valuable to conduct in-depth research on the photoluminescence characteristics through using UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. 0.5 mg of compound 1 is dispersed in different organic solvents, and each of which contained 3 mL of ethanol (EtOH), isopropanol (IPA), acetylacetone (AC), Dichloromethane (DCM), ethylene glycol (EG), methanol (MeOH), acetonitrile (CH3CN), N, N′-dimethylacetamide (DMA), and H2O (Fig. S2). According to the experimental results, fluorescence detection is performed in H2O. Furthermore, the optimum emission peak is aware at 457 nm when excited at 395 nm. The absorption band at 270 nm is mainly attributed to the π-π* transition of CC in the sp2 hybridization of aromatic compounds [44], while the peak 330 nm is attributing to the n-π* transitions of the viologen functional groups of SO and 4,4′-bipyridine in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 4 a) [45]. The intense blue fluorescence of the aqueous solution of compound 1 is displayed under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 4a inset). The wavelength-independent fluorescence capability of compound 1 is exhibited in Fig. 4b and the emission wavelength is not shifted by regulating the excitation wavelength from 340 nm to 400 nm. It is proven that the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength is excitation-independent. The fluorescence decay curve of compound 1 is equivalent to the average lifetime of 9.51 ns, which is composed of fast component τ1 = 2.20 ns and slow component τ2 = 14.41 ns. It is pointed out that the fluorescence lifetime of compound 1 is mainly controlled by the functional groups of viologen ligands (Fig. 4c) [46].

Fluorescence stability is one of the essential factors that an excellent fluorescent probe should possess. As shown in Fig. 4d, the stability of compound 1 is excellent within 60 days and the fluorescence intensity did not change significantly, which proves that compound 1 is an ideal fluorescent probe with long-term anti-interference. In addition, biocompatibility is one of the important factors. Therefore, 0–1 mol⋅L−1 NaCl solutions are selected to simulate the internal environment of organisms and the result shows that no significant change is observed in the fluorescence intensity of compound 1 (Fig. S3). The fluorescence intensity of compound 1 at pH = 4−8 remains basically unchanged, signifying that the structure is stable in weakly acidic and neutral conditions (Fig. 4e). More importantly, compound 1 maintains strong fluorescence emission to persist 10 min under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 4f) and Xenon lamp with excitation wavelength of 1500 mW cm−2 (Fig. S4). The temperature is also an important external factor. The fluorescence intensity of compound 1 at 25−50 °C is not interfered (Fig. S5). It is proved that compound 1 can be used as a sensor with excellent optical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization parameter and fluorescence response to Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: According to the presence of a quantity of SO and pyridine N atoms on the surface of compound 1 and previous study on the coordination of Ag+ with sulfonic acid groups, we speculate that compound 1 may react with Ag+ [47]. In order to verify this hypothesis, the solution of compound 1 is mixed with different interference cation solutions (5 μM) to check their selectivity, including Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Cu+, Hg2+, Pd2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+, Sn4+. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity of compound 1 is substantial quenched in the presence of only targeting Ag+, which means that compound 1 has outstanding selectivity for Ag+. In accordance with expectation, it has an extremely fast response time of 2 s and can remain stable within 60 s (Fig. 5b). It can be proved that this fluorescent probe has excellent sensitivity. The impact of environmental temperature on Ag+ detection of compound 1 is negligible (Fig. 5c). Therefore, the optimal temperature is 25 °C (room temperature), which is easier to achieve. Ag+ usually exists in the form of AgOH precipitation under alkaline conditions, so it is difficult to react with compounds. To further optimize parameters, a mixture of compound 1 and Ag+ is staying in solutions of different pH, which ensures a perfect response to Ag+ at pH = 7.2 (Fig. 5d). In summary, the optimal parameters of the sensing system are reaction time of 60 s, room temperature (25 °C) and pH = 7.2 of aqueous solution.

When the Ag+ concentration increases from 0 to 20 μM, the fluorescence intensity of compound 1 decreases continuously at 457 nm (Fig. 5e). Moreover, a positive proportional linear relationship (R2 = 0.9984) is obtained between the fluorescence ratio I/I0 and Ag+ concentration in the range of 0.1–3 μM (Fig. 5f). The LOD of compound 1 toward Ag+ is calculated to be 0.0411 μM through the formula of 3σ/k (σ is the standard error of three parallel experiments, and k is the slope of the fitting curve). This is not only superior to the previous works (Table S4), but also lower than the allowable concentration of Ag+ in potable water (460 nM) specified by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [14]. There is a certain significance for the evaluation of trace silver ions in water environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of compound 1 to Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: Even if the experimental results indicate that compound 1 can recognize Ag+ linearly, the mechanism of Ag+ recognition should be further explored by PXRD, FT-IR, Raman, UV–vis, fluorescence lifetime and XPS spectra. Firstly, the fluorescence lifetime of compound 1 decreases from 9.51 ns to 4.12 ns with the addition of Ag+, representing a dynamic quenching process (Fig. 6 a) [48]. As shown in Fig. S6, quenching constant (Ksv) is 8.4 × 105 M−1, which clearly demonstrates the strong interaction between Ag+ and compound 1. Secondly, the PXRD of compound 1 is changed after adding Ag+, which may be due to the coordination between functional groups on the crystal skeleton and Ag+ (Fig. 3a) [49]. Additionally, the UV–vis spectrum can analyze the coordination reaction between Ag+ and compound 1, a new broad peak appeared at around 760 nm, which is a new peak of Ag−O (Fig. 6b) [50]. The peak at 330 nm is significantly weakened after Ag+ treatment, which directly indicates that the addition of Ag+ leads to collapse of the conjugated structure of the internal chromophore in compound 1 [51], which is consistent with the results of PXRD. Then, in the FT-IR spectrum, the stretching vibration of N−H significantly decreases at 3325 cm−1 [52], and the peak at SO at 1151 and 1298 cm−1 disappears significantly. However, a new peak representing Ag−O appears at 594 cm−1 [53], which supports the coordination of Ag+ with pyridine N atoms and SO (Fig. 3b). The same conclusion can be obtained by the Raman spectrum. The characteristic stretching vibration peaks of SO bond at 1142, 1168 and 1292 cm−1 disappear, which indicates that there is a coordination reaction between SO and Ag+ (Fig. S1) [54]. Finally, the XPS spectra can also confirm the above conclusions. The characteristic peaks of Ag can be seen at 373.2 (Ag3d3/2) and 367.1 (Ag3d5/2) eV, indicating the existence of Ag+ (Fig. 6c and d). The peak position of N1s changes from 398.1 to 398.6 eV, and its intensity decreases by about 40 %. The new peak at 401.7 eV can be attributed to Ag−N, indicating that Ag+ has coordinated with pyridine N atoms (Fig. 6e, S8) [55]. The O1s is similar to the N1s, the peak position shifts from 531.8 to 532.1 eV and the intensity decreases by 60 %. A new peak of Ag−O is fitted at 530.26 eV (Fig. 6f, S9) [56]. The calculated strong binding constant (Ka) of Ag+ coordination with compound 1 is 6.1 × 105 M−1 also supporting our hypothesis (Fig. S10). The fluorescence quenching mechanism of compound 1 is summarized: the coordination of functional groups (pyridine N atoms and SO) on the surface of compound 1 with Ag+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of Ag+ in practical samples
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the residual Ag+ in real water samples often causes heavy metal pollution, and the concentration of Ag+ affects the application of antibacterial disinfectant spray. Therefore, it is of great significance to evaluate the ability of this probe to detect Ag+ in real water samples (lake water, tap water) and disinfection sprays. Different concentrations of Ag+ (0, 42, 45, 50 nM/mL) are added to these samples. Subsequently, the developed supramolecular fluorescent probe is used to determine the concentrations of Ag+ in the blank and actual samples. The recovery rates of the supramolecular fluorescent probe are 99.3–104.1 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) is less than 4 %, which shows the high accuracy and practicability of detecting Ag+ (Table 1 ). Hence, these results indicate that the supramolecular fluorescent probe we designed is suitable for the quantitative detection of Ag+ in practical samples.

In order to intuitively and simply quantitative analysis Ag+, a fluorescent hydrogel sensor for visual detection of Ag+ is prepared. Compound 1 can be encapsulated in a hydrogel with a unique 3D structure as a solid-state fluorescence sensor for detecting Ag+. The blank hydrogel synthesized with AAM and ammonium persulfate is colorless under natural light, while compound 1+hydrogel has intense blue fluorescence (Fig. 7 a). In order to prove the ability of compound 1+hydrogel to detect trace Ag+, it is found that the fluorescence response of compound 1+hydrogel soaked in Ag+ aqueous solution is significantly quenched (Fig. S11). Furthermore, Ag+ aqueous solution with different concentrations (0.1−5 μM) is added to compound 1+hydrogel for 2 min, and then dry it until there are no water drops on the surface of the hydrogel. Under the irradiation of natural light or 365 nm UV light, the images are photographed by smartphones, which are identified and compared by Adobe Color software and the HSV or RGB values are identified in turn. The discoloration of the hydrogel sensor can be recognized by naked eyes, and it can be found that the hydrogel changes from yellow to dark brown under different Ag+ concentrations (Fig. 7b). The fluorescence color of hydrogel changes from bright blue to dark blue, indicating that the fluorescence intensity is significantly reduced under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 7c). It can be seen that the H/V value and Ag+ concentration of the solution (0.1−5 μM) is a good linear relationship within the 0.1−4 μM range (y = 0.2351x+1.8269, R2 = 0.9962), with a LOD of 0.0332 μM (Fig. 7d). More importantly, the chromaticity of the hydrogel sensor containing compound 1 is tracked for one month, and it is concluded that its storage capacity is very prominent over 30 days (Fig. 7e). The results can prove that the compound 1+hydrogel is an excellent choice for extracting Ag+ in daily life. Compound 1+hydrogel can also be used for sensing of Ag+ in natural light, while the R/G value of the hydrogel sensor can be determined at the same time. Linear relationship is observed within the range of 0.1–3 μM (y = 0.4181x+1.2851, R2 = 0.9884), with a LOD of 0.0404 μM (Fig. S12). The supposed fluorescence reaction mechanism of hydrogel is roughly as follows: When compound 1+hydrogel is placed in the aqueous solution of Ag+, the unique 3D hydrogel network can promote the enrichment of Ag+. Meanwhile, compound 1 with the rich functional groups encapsulated in these 3D network structures acts as a fluorescence phosphor and responds quickly to Ag+ [50]. The fluorescence effect of compound 1 in the 3D structure of hydrogel is obviously better than that of its own 2D hydrogen bond network, so it can be inferred that the dimensional change will also affect the sensing of Ag+ [57]. Furthermore, the visible detection of Ag+ in compound 1+hydrogel system at different temperatures (4 °C and 50 °C) is studied (Fig. S13). However, there is also no significant difference in the response color of the compound 1+hydrogel sensor at different Ag+ concentrations compared with that at room temperature (4 °C and 50 °C), which shows that the compound 1+hydrogel sensor has strong environmental temperature adaptability. The comparative experimental results of compound 1 and compound 1+hydrogel show that the intelligent hydrogel fluorescence sensing platform assisted by smartphone is outstanding and can measure the target Ag+ quickly and accurately.

On this basis, compound 1+hydrogel is operated to monitor the concentration of Ag+ in the commercial disinfection sprays. The hydrogel sensor is soaked in disinfectant spray solution diluted by 100 times for 2 min, and the concentration of Ag+ is visually observed according to the R/G or H/V observed by the hydrogel. The observed results are shown in Table 2 and S5. Compound 1+hydrogel has an excellent recovery rate (102.34–105.55 %) for the detection of Ag+, and the RSD is less than 6 %. The above results prove that compound 1+hydrogel can identify the concentration of Ag+ by visual inspection, and its portability is suitable for human life.

In summary, a POM/viologen supramolecular fluorescent probe [(HMSBP)2(γ-Mo8O26)]·4H2O (1) has been synthesized by hydrothermal method (MSBP = 1-(4-Methanesulfonyl-benzyl)-[4,4']bipyridinyl-1-ium). Compound 1 has the unique supramolecular structure, rich functional groups, good optical stability and fluorescence intensity, which can be regarded as a supramolecular fluorescence probe for selectively targeted detection of Ag+ (LOD is 0.0411 μM with a linearity range of 0.1–3 μM). The concentration of Ag+ in environmental water samples and disinfectant spray on the market is quantitatively detected by this probe, and the recovery rate and precision are satisfactory. In addition, compound 1+hydrogel based on intelligent sensing system has also been successfully developed, which can capture the fluorescence discoloration on hydrogel by Adobe Color software to quantitatively determine the concentration of Ag+ in real scenes. This study not only invents a novel and simple strategy to detect Ag+ with a POM/viologen supramolecular fluorescent probe, but also provides a feasible way for qualitative/semi-quantitative detection of visual fluorescent probes.

Kai Li: Data curation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Tao Liu: Formal analysis. Jun Ying: Investigation, Validation. Aixiang Tian: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xiuli Wang: Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports of this research by Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21571023, 21971024, 21671025), Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC1807049, XLYC1902011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (LJKMZ20221479) and Department of Science and Technology of Liaoning Province (2022-MS-369).
